matches.match_rationale: The core pain is a manual, multi-step workflow: overtime/bonus claims arrive, get calculated, routed for approval, then disbursed and documented. Each of these steps is a discrete action with branching logic (approval granted vs. rejected, exit pay vs. bonus), making it a textbook task automation target. Education operators with 500+ staff will have dozens of these claims per pay cycle, and the repetition justifies an automated agent pipeline.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An agent that ingests supplementary pay claims (from timesheets, HR submissions, or email), validates inputs against policy rules, routes to the correct approver, tracks status centrally, and triggers disbursement documentation — reducing manual touchpoints from ~8 to ~2 per claim.

matches.match_rationale: Supplementary payroll items typically arrive in heterogeneous formats — spreadsheet submissions, HR system exports, manager emails, or paper timesheets scanned to PDF. Normalizing these semi-structured inputs into a canonical schema (employee ID, claim type, amount, period, approver) is a prerequisite for any downstream automation or tracking. This capability directly addresses the 'no single source of truth' sub-pain.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A transformation layer that ingests claim data from multiple input channels and maps them to a standardized payroll item schema, enabling a unified status dashboard and eliminating the reconciliation step that currently causes backlogs.

matches.match_rationale: When claims arrive as unstructured text (manager emails, resignation letters triggering final pay, bonus justification memos), extracting the relevant structured attributes — employee name, claim type, amount, dates, approval chain — is a direct fit. This removes the manual data-entry step that contributes to backlog accumulation.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An extraction layer that parses incoming payroll claim emails or documents and pre-populates structured claim records, cutting claim entry time from minutes to seconds and reducing transcription errors.

matches.match_rationale: Final pay delays cause 'staff relations friction' — the operator must communicate delays, explain calculations, and document decisions to departing staff. Drafting these communications (final pay breakdowns, delay notices, bonus award letters) is repetitive and time-consuming for HR teams. AI text generation can produce compliant, personalized letters from structured payroll data with minimal human editing.
matches.opportunity_sketch: Auto-generated final pay explanation letters and delay notifications, produced from structured payroll records and reviewed by HR before sending — reducing drafting time and ensuring consistent, professional communication to departing employees.

matches.match_rationale: Incoming supplementary pay requests often need triage: is this an overtime claim, a bonus nomination, an allowance adjustment, or a final pay trigger? Misclassification at intake routes claims to the wrong approver and adds delay. Text classification on submission text or email subject lines can auto-route claims to the correct workflow branch, reducing the manual triage step.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An intake classifier that reads claim submission text and tags it with the correct claim type and routing path, automatically assigning it to the right approver queue and reducing misrouting-related delays.

matches.match_rationale: Education operators have layered policy documents governing overtime eligibility, bonus criteria, allowance rates, and final pay entitlements — often across enterprise agreements, government funding agreements, and internal HR policies. HR staff and managers currently must manually cross-reference these to answer 'is this claim valid?' RAG over the policy corpus can answer entitlement questions grounded in the actual documents, reducing approval bottlenecks caused by policy uncertainty.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A policy Q&A assistant where approvers ask 'Is an employee on contract type X entitled to this overtime rate?' and receive a cited, grounded answer from the enterprise agreement and HR policy documents — eliminating the manual policy lookup step in the approval workflow.
